The white crust ahead is split by hairline cracks that do not match the wind. When the party crosses, a giant scorpion erupts from the hardpan with claws already raised.
Shapes waver beside a line of ruined stones, vanishing whenever the wind rises. The bandits wait until the glare hides their drawstrings.
Giant vultures hop along the rim of a dry wash, bold from hunger and carrion. They take wing only long enough to dive at anything that looks weak.
The dunes have erased every trail except a line of wind-carved ridges. Reading their shadows points toward water before the sun moves too far.
A black ridge of sun-baked stone radiates heat through boots and gloves. Crossing it quickly hurts less than stopping halfway.
A city of green towers trembles on the horizon, perfect and unreachable. It fades into ordinary dust when approached, leaving only a cooler breath of wind.
Only the top of a carved route marker shows above a dune. Digging it free reveals an old warning and the direction of a road the desert swallowed.
Faint camel bells ring from beyond a ridge where no caravan can be seen. The sound follows the party until someone chooses to answer it.
A sun-black obelisk has split down the middle, and the shadow inside points the wrong way. Locals want it sealed before nightfall.
Red fruit grows high on a many-spined cactus, sweet enough to be worth the trouble. The ripest pieces sit where the spines are densest.
A fan of bitter aloe hides in the shade of a cracked boulder. Clean cuts preserve the gel before the leaves dry in the wind.
Dry pods rattle beneath low scrub where the sand holds a trace of moisture. The seeds are small, filling, and easily lost to the wind.
Small fish hide under reeds in a shaded oasis pool. Nets must be worked gently so the mud does not foul the only clean water nearby.
A rain-cut wadi still holds a deep pocket of brown water beneath overhanging stone. Catfish wait at the bottom where the pool is coolest.
A shallow salt pool flickers with tiny brine shrimp after rare rain. The harvest is quick work before the sun thickens the water again.
Green stains mark a copper seam exposed along a dry cliff face. The rock is brittle, so controlled strikes matter more than force.
Wind has scraped a salt pan into clean geometric plates. The best slabs lift whole if separated before the edge crumbles.
Golden pebbles glitter in the bed of a vanished stream. Sorting true sunstone from common quartz takes patience in the glare.
A dead acacia limb has seasoned hard in the open air. Its thorns make the work slow, but the heartwood burns hot and clean.
A palm has fallen beside the oasis with its trunk still mostly sound. Cutting it without fouling the water requires careful staging.
A half-buried cargo crate creaks beneath the sand, its owner long gone. The planks can be salvaged if the shifting dune is braced first.
A lone traveler waves from the heat shimmer while jackal laughter circles behind the dunes. The mirage breaks only when the trap closes.
A dune face collapses to reveal glassy claw marks and a hot electrical smell. The dragon is not here, but its territorial servants are.
A half-buried stone head grinds awake when shadows touch its brow. Sand hardens around it into a crude guardian body.
Old caravaners carved star marks into sun-bleached stones. Reading them correctly points toward water instead of a beautiful false horizon.
A fast squall lifts glassy sand into a glittering wall. Dropping low and covering exposed skin keeps the storm from flensing the careless.
A ring of date palms hides a clear spring beneath woven reed mats. Drinking slowly restores strength without shocking a heat-sick body.
An oasis shows no reflections in its water, not even the sun. A caravan guide swears the pool reflected a missing child yesterday.
A dead pack camel carries a map tube sealed in blue wax. The route inside leads to an untouched ruin and marks one dune as 'listening'.
Wind strips sand from a black stone door covered in old floating-city sigils. Someone has recently oiled the hinges from the inside.
A water merchant offers payment for finding a lost cistern key before a rival caravan reaches the same dunes. The debt is older than the merchant admits.
Blue fire crawls along a ridge each night and points toward a collapsed observatory. The fire fades whenever spoken prayers begin.
A squat date palm carries fruit high above thorny skirts. The sweetest dates hang where the trunk bends over hot stone.
A barrel cactus hides clean water behind hooked spines. Cutting the heart without wasting the plant takes practiced hands.
Tiny cracks around saltbush roots reveal buried truffles. They dry quickly once exposed to the noon heat.
Tiny silverfish flash under lily pads in the oasis shallows. They vanish into root tangles at the first heavy step.
Small mud crabs shelter where reeds shade the spring outflow. A basket can catch them before the water clouds.
A dusk sand eel swims through loose sand near an old riverbed, leaving ripples like water. It strikes hooks baited with dates.
Sunstone chips glitter in a dry wash after wind strips away the dust. The brightest pieces are brittle and hot to the touch.
Lightning or old magic has fused a dune into green-black glass. Useful blades can be lifted from the vein if wrapped at once.
A dead acacia twists over a wash, hard as bone and almost free of rot. Its thorns remain dangerous even after death.
A broken caravan axle lies beside old wheel ruts. The hardwood is scarred but strong enough for repairs.
Dry palm ribs from an abandoned shelter can be bundled into light framing. The trick is finding pieces not eaten by beetles.
Green-flecked stones shimmer in a dry wash where heat bends the air. The real copper nodules are colder than the false glints around them.
